flashcards.front: What three properties define an equivalence relation?
flashcards.back: Reflexive (aRa), Symmetric (aRb ⇒ bRa), and Transitive (aRb ∧ bRc ⇒ aRc)

flashcards.front: How does a partial order differ from an equivalence relation?
flashcards.back: Partial orders require antisymmetry (aRb ∧ bRa ⇒ a=b) instead of symmetry
Set of elements equivalent to a given element under the relation: [a] = {x | xRa}

flashcards.front: Name a real-world application of partial orders
flashcards.back: Task scheduling in operating systems (dependency management)

flashcards.front: What mathematical structure represents equivalence classes?
flashcards.back: Partition of the set into disjoint subsets

flashcards.front: Which relation property is violated if a < b but b ≮ a in a poset?
What visual tool represents partial orders?
flashcards.back: Hasse diagram (shows hierarchical relationships without redundant edges)

flashcards.front: How does modular arithmetic demonstrate equivalence?
flashcards.back: a ≡ b mod n forms equivalence classes (congruence classes)

flashcards.front: What's the dual concept of greatest lower bound in posets?
flashcards.back: Least upper bound (meet and join operations)

flashcards.front: Why are equivalence relations crucial in databases?
flashcards.back: Enable data normalization through partitioning into distinct categories

flashcards.front: What makes a relation a strict partial order?
How do equivalence relations help in graph theory?
flashcards.back: Identify connected components in undirected graphs
